text: During the laparoscopic esophagectomy, the surgeon moves towards the chest after initially dissecting the stomach. The diaphragm is visible in the upper part of the field. The liver is retracted, and the surgeon is grasping the esophagus, cutting alongside it to clear surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate tissues around the gullet, with a fan retractor holding back the liver. This dissection frees the passage of the gullet as it descends from the chest to the abdomen.
The surgeon continues dissection into the mediastinum, revealing the pulsating aorta below, which is the large artery descending from the heart into the chest.

The surgeon continues the dissection to free the esophagus from surrounding tissues, carefully moving the diaphragmatic surface of the heart away from the esophagus. The pulsatile heart is visible as the esophagus is progressively freed, revealing the entire tube. The dissection proceeds towards the upper end, transitioning to the neck area for further removal of the esophagus.

The laparoscopic view shows the mobilized esophagus being pulled out from the neck, along with part of the stomach. The newly created artificial tube from the stomach is stitched to the remaining esophagus above, completing the thoracoscopic esophagectomy.
